full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I started my Women's Bible study last week.<|3.26|> <|3.92|> We are exploring the role of women in Luke's gospel,<|7.88|> <|8.30|> and we will meet monthly through April.<|10.18|> <|10.86|> Any of you ladies who would like to join us, let me know.<|13.56|> <|14.00|> It is not too late.<|15.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So in our first gathering, I was sharing that it's important to understand some of the overarching<|7.30|> <|7.30|> themes when you're studying a gospel and how to identify that writer's particular markers.<|16.40|> <|16.40|> You see, when I was in seminary, I had a New Testament professor who said that unless we<|21.84|> <|21.84|> were raised in a denomination that made you memorize scripture, we were in need of some<|27.74|> <|27.74|> tricks.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We needed to be able to find the context clues.<|3.50|> <|3.50|> And of course, I was raised an Episcopalian,<|6.50|> <|6.50|> so I needed many tricks, lots of clues.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> So we needed to understand the shorthand<|14.50|> <|14.50|> of each gospel writer's theme<|17.00|> <|17.00|> and what their fingerprints were<|19.00|> <|19.00|> that could help us make an educated guess<|22.00|> <|22.00|> whenever we saw a piece of scripture out of context.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is no doubting that this week's gospel is from Mark.<|6.76|> <|6.76|> You could give me a pop quiz and I would pass it right away, and I'll tell you why.<|12.74|> <|12.74|> Of all the gospels, the disciples in Mark are the most pitiful.<|20.74|> <|20.74|> Mark has no patience for them, and they get a full edit every time.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So we just heard Deacon Sally read,<|2.72|> <|2.72|> "Jesus and his disciples passed through Galilee.<|8.66|> <|8.66|> He did not want anyone to know it, for he was teaching his disciples, saying to them,<|14.24|> <|14.24|> 'The Son of Man is to be betrayed into human hands, and they will kill him, and three days<|19.62|> <|19.62|> after being killed he will rise again.'"<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Here's how you know it's Mark.<|2.98|> <|2.98|> But they did not understand what he was saying,<|6.88|> <|6.88|> and they were afraid to ask him.<|10.24|> <|10.24|> This is the second time in a very brief span of time<|15.68|> <|15.68|> that Jesus has told them the same thing.<|18.76|> <|18.76|> He has told them of his suffering.<|20.82|> <|20.82|> He has told them of his death and his resurrection.<|25.00|> <|25.00|> I mean, we just heard it a week ago.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and they still don't understand.<|3.70|> <|5.42|> Unlike Mark, I actually have some sympathy for the disciples,<|9.18|> <|9.58|> for their lack of understanding.<|10.96|> <|11.62|> It makes no sense.<|13.04|> <|13.80|> It is unimaginable to them that Jesus could meet such a fate.<|18.82|> <|19.76|> I would have struggled to wrap my head around it too.<|22.30|> <|22.30|> But Mark is not kind to the disciples.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The part I don't understand is that they didn't ask any questions.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> The disciples and Mark are not only slow on the uptake,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> they also seem to be moving around in a constant state of fear.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we haven't seen much to give credence to the fact that they should be afraid to ask questions.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> We are told that this is a time of teaching.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> Who doesn't love questions if you're a teacher?<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So instead, I would love to know what would have happened had they asked.<|7.20|> <|7.20|> "Jesus, you keep talking about this betrayal, this death, this rising.<|12.88|> <|12.88|> What does that mean?<|14.78|> <|14.78|> When will it happen?<|16.86|> <|16.86|> What does it have to do with us?"<|19.44|> <|19.44|> And perhaps the most important question they could have asked, "Why does it have to happen<|24.96|> <|24.96|> this way?"<|26.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.80|> Instead, they keep their mouths shut.<|3.00|> <|3.00|> They do not ask any questions of substance,<|6.50|> <|6.50|> and then instead, they start an inane argument about who is the greatest.<|13.00|> <|14.00|> It reminds me of those topper conversations that children have<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My favorite was overheard while driving a carpool once.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> One child hopped in the car and said,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> "Hey, we just went to Disney World."<|10.00|> <|10.00|> And another child who was already in the car said,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> "Oh yeah, I live at Disney World."<|15.00|> <|17.00|> That's a true story.<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I think Mark's pitiful disciples seem no better.<|5.60|> <|5.60|> What if they had asked real questions?<|9.70|> <|9.70|> What if they had shown real curiosity about what was coming and about Jesus?<|18.90|> <|18.90|> How might their journey with Jesus have been different, been enriched?<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And maybe it is because I was a teacher, or maybe I became a teacher because, chicken<|7.00|> <|7.00|> or the egg, I am curious.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> I ask a lot of questions.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> I love questions.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> And I wonder about many things, usually out loud, and sometimes to the dismay of my colleagues<|22.00|> <|22.00|> at staff meetings.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I love the conversation, the research, the discovery that come with questions.<|6.98|> <|8.24|> And I think God does too.<|10.42|> <|12.30|> We baptized all sorts of folks during the pandemic.<|16.36|> <|16.76|> It was one of the joys that our sacramental ministry continued even when so much else in our lives had slowed.<|24.88|> <|24.88|> It was one of the joys that our sacramental ministry continued even when so much else in our lives had slowed.<|25.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We baptized many babies, lots of children, and even a few adults<|6.50|> <|6.50|> out in the white garth on Sunday afternoons,<|10.00|> <|10.00|> surrounded by family and friends.<|13.00|> <|14.00|> And last April, it was my turn, and I baptized three brothers.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> It was amazing.<|20.50|> <|20.50|> And one of those boys was incredibly curious.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I love that his brothers call him Ninja,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> and that actually has nothing to do with the story,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> but I think Ninja is an awesome nickname.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> So this seven-year-old boy, Ninja,<|13.00|> <|13.00|> wanted to know exactly what we were about to do and why.<|18.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He wanted to inspect the font, he wanted to check out the oil,<|4.20|> <|4.20|> he smelled it, he wanted to know about the candle,<|7.20|> <|7.20|> and he wanted to know exactly how much water I was going to use.<|12.00|> <|13.20|> He also asked how long I had been doing this.<|16.20|> <|17.00|> And I think that's a really smart question,<|20.20|> <|20.20|> because if some lady is going to pour oil and water over your head,<|24.70|> <|24.70|> you would rather this not be her first time.<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So one of the really nice things about these, for those of you who have hair my color, will<|9.68|> <|9.68|> remember the 1928 prayer book version of private baptism, was that there was time for all of<|20.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There was plenty of time for questions to be asked and answered.<|4.70|> <|4.70|> And so that's what we did.<|6.20|> <|6.20|> We didn't start until he was satisfied that he had gotten the whole scoop.<|11.12|> <|11.12|> And afterwards, we marveled at the immiscible way<|16.72|> <|16.72|> in which the chrism oil pooled in bubbles on top of the holy water in the font.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I mean, it was curiosity about God and science and baptism all rolled into one.<|7.30|> <|7.90|> It was awesome.<|8.66|> <|10.96|> We write notes to each of the children that we baptize.<|15.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We thank them for the privilege of being with them in this holy moment.<|4.44|> <|4.96|> We welcome them into God's family,<|7.38|> <|8.06|> and we tell them how excited we are<|10.76|> <|10.76|> that we'll get to see them grow up at St. John's.<|14.50|> <|15.52|> And so I wrote a note to Ninja, and I said all those things.<|18.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I also told him that I loved his questions and that God loved them too.<|5.86|> <|6.52|> And I told him to never stop asking, to never stop being curious, because God loves our curiosity.<|14.96|> <|16.22|> Curiosity is holy, and it is how our faith grows.<|20.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.04|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And these poor, clueless, Markan disciples,<|4.12|> <|4.96|> what can we learn from them?<|6.42|> <|7.96|> I think we can learn that we should not fear asking questions.<|11.22|> <|12.10|> We should trust that God invites and delights in our curiosity.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> community.<|1.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Questions do not mean we are stupid.<|3.50|> <|3.50|> They do not mean we are skeptical.<|6.50|> <|6.50|> And they do not have to mean we doubt.<|10.50|> <|10.50|> Curiosity is simply how our faith grows.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And this is a great time to be curious at St. John's.<|4.90|> <|4.90|> The formation year is starting and there are countless ways to engage your holy curiosity.<|14.36|> <|14.36|> Are there questions that you've been holding on to?<|20.00|> <|20.00|> Questions that you don't think you could or should ask God?<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How might our journey be different with Jesus<|4.86|> <|4.86|> if we asked those questions?<|7.06|> <|7.34|> How might it be enriched?<|9.14|> <|10.94|> I encourage you.<|12.02|> <|12.82|> Go ahead.<|13.52|> <|14.54|> Ask.<|15.06|>
